content:
Here is a concise step‐by‐step solution:

1.  Let P(n) = product of the decimal digits of n, and set up the equation  
        P(n) = n² − 15n − 27.  
2.  If n has any zero‐digit then P(n)=0, but n²−15n−27=0 has no integer solution.  Hence all digits of n are in {1,…,9}, so P(n)≥1.  
3.  For 1-digit n (1≤n≤9) the equation becomes  
        n = n² −15n −27  ⇒  n² −16n −27=0  
    which has no integer root.  
4.  For 3-digit (or larger) n, say n≥100, the right‐hand side grows like n² and quickly exceeds the maximal digit‐product 9³=729, so no solutions occur for n≥100.  
5.  The only remaining case is 2-digit n = 10a + b with a,b∈{1,…,9}.  One finds the single solution  
        n = 17,  since 1·7 = 17² −15·17 −27 = 7.  
Therefore the only positive solution is n=17, and the requested sum is

\boxed{17}

        n = 17,  since 1·7 = 17² −15·17 −27 = 7.

\boxed{17}
